title: Some People Are Better at Eavesdropping Than Others
author: Jake Currie
text: Subscribe to skip adsAdvertisementNeuroscienceSome People Are Better at Eavesdropping Than OthersNew study shows what happens in our brains as we track multiple conversationsJCBy Jake Currie4:00 PM CDT on July 16, 2026Share on FacebookShare on X (formerly Twitter)Share on RedditShare on EmailShare on BlueskyYou’re talking to someone at a crowded restaurant when their eyes unexpectedly widen. “Did you hear that?” they ask. It turns out that, while listening to you, they also caught a dramatic bit of the conversation from a couple at a nearby table. According to a new study published in PLOS Biology, we all have this ability to track multiple conversations at once, even in noisy surroundings, but some people are better at it than others. Subscribe to skip adsFeatured VideoPowered By 10 Is This Jellyfish Immortal? Researchers Think SoShareNextStayMost previous research into how boisterous environments affect our attention has explored how people tune in, sustain their focus, and screen out the noise. This recent study, conducted by an international team of neuroscientists, investigates how we shift our attention between two competing sources. The researchers strapped EEG harnesses around participants’ heads while they listened to two simultaneous recordings of TED Talks amid background noise. The subjects were then tasked with swapping their attention between the two speakers as the researchers recorded their neural activity. Read more: “What Searchable Speech Will Do To You”Already have an account?Log inJoin to continue readingYou've run out of free articles.
